title: Listening - Part 3: The Virtual Arena
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a psychologist, Dr. Marcus Thorne, and a game designer, Elena Varga, about the impact of video games on society. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing why games are addictive and how they can be used for positive social change.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Marcus, gaming is no longer a basement hobby. It’s the world’s biggest entertainment industry. Why do games have such a grip on us?

Marcus: It’s the feedback loop. [15]Unlike real life, where efforts often take years to bear fruit, games offer 'micro-rewards' every few seconds.[/15] Your brain gets a dopamine hit for every monster defeated or level gained. It creates a sense of agency and competence that many people feel is missing from their professional lives.

Interviewer: Elena, you design 'serious games'. Can play actually solve real problems?

Elena: [16]I’m frustrated by the idea that games are just for 'escaping' reality; I think they are the best tool we have for 'practicing' reality.[/16] We’ve built simulators that help people understand climate change by letting them manage a city’s energy for a century. When you 'fail' in a game, you learn; when you fail in real policy-making, the consequences are permanent.

Interviewer: Marcus, there’s a lot of concern about 'gaming disorder'. Is it a real medical issue?

Marcus: [17]I believe we have to be careful not to pathologize a passion, but yes, for a small percentage, it becomes a maladaptive coping mechanism.[/17] They aren't playing for fun; they're playing to numb themselves from real-world anxiety. The game isn't the problem—the underlying trauma is. We should treat the person, not just take away the console.

Interviewer: Elena, you’ve been vocal about 'monetization' in games. What’s your worry?

Elena: [18]I find the rise of 'loot boxes' and gambling-like mechanics in games aimed at children to be morally indefensible.[/18] We are essentially training young brains to associate dopamine with spending money. It’s predatory, and it’s a far cry from the creative, skill-based games I grew up with.

Interviewer: Marcus, what about the social aspect? Is gaming making us lonelier?

Marcus: [19]The data suggests the opposite—virtual worlds are becoming the new 'town squares' for a generation that doesn't go to malls or parks.[/19] They are forming deep, meaningful friendships across borders. The challenge is that these spaces are owned by corporations, not the public, which changes the nature of the interaction.

Interviewer: Finally, what’s the future of gaming?

Elena: [20]I suspect we will move away from 'screens' entirely toward mixed-reality experiences where the game world is layered over our physical one.[/20] Imagine a city where history pops up as you walk down the street. Marcus and I are both excited—and a little nervous—about a world where the line between 'play' and 'life' finally disappears.

questions.question: Marcus believes that games are addictive because they
options: A) require years of effort before showing results.
B) provide immediate positive reinforcement for actions.
C) help people avoid feelings of agency and competence.
D) are more professional than the average person's life.
questions.explanation: He mentions 'micro-rewards every few seconds' that provide a dopamine hit.

questions.question: What is Elena’s view on the purpose of 'serious games'?
options: A) They are primarily for escaping the difficulties of reality.
B) They allow users to experience failure without real-world risk.
C) They should not be used for understanding climate change.
D) They are more dangerous than actual policy-making.
questions.explanation: She argues games are for 'practicing reality' where failure leads to learning rather than permanent consequences.

questions.question: Regarding 'gaming disorder', Marcus argues that
options: A) most passionate players should be medically diagnosed.
B) the game itself is usually the cause of a person's trauma.
C) we should focus on the mental health issues behind the behavior.
D) taking away a person's console is the best form of treatment.
questions.explanation: He says the game isn't the problem—the 'underlying trauma' is, and we should 'treat the person'.

questions.question: Elena objects to modern monetization because it
options: A) encourages children to develop creative skills.
B) is too similar to the games she played as a child.
C) links psychological rewards to financial spending.
D) makes games too difficult for young players.
questions.explanation: She claims 'loot boxes' train brains to associate 'dopamine with spending money'.

questions.question: What does Marcus say about the social impact of gaming?
options: A) It is making people feel lonelier than in the past.
B) It has turned parks into virtual malls for the youth.
C) It provides a digital space for people to build relationships.
D) It is better than the town squares owned by the public.
questions.explanation: He describes virtual worlds as the new 'town squares' where people form deep friendships.

questions.question: Elena predicts that in the future, gaming will
options: A) be restricted to computer screens and traditional consoles.
B) become a completely separate part of a person's life.
C) integrate digital elements into the real world.
D) prevent people from ever walking down a street.
questions.explanation: She foresees 'mixed-reality experiences where the game world is layered over our physical one'.
